extracted_information: The Hemp Beverage Alliance recommends regulatory principles for a safe, thriving, and transparent hemp beverage industry, with a strong focus on age restriction (21+).

features.category: Regulatory Principles (Recommended by Hemp Beverage Alliance)
items: Age restriction: Sale of cannabinoid-infused beverages restricted to adults aged 21 years or older.
Serving size and potency: THC levels should be safe, effective, and provide value.
Labeling: Clear, consistent labeling to empower consumers and build credibility.
Regulatory clarity: Regulations should be thoughtful, concise, support consumer safety and industry growth.
Marketplace consistency: State governments should work together to develop consistent guidelines.

items.details: The sale of cannabinoid-infused beverages should be restricted to adults aged 21 years or older.

details: Labels must clearly indicate through text and symbol that the product is for use only by adults aged 21 years or older.
Packages should be tamper-evident and consistent with current alcohol containers.
Hemp beverages should be sold only to adults aged 21 years or older.
Age-verification required prior to sales.
Retailers should be licensed to ensure compliance in selling only to adults aged 21 years or older.
Retailers should be trained to understand age-gating hemp beverages, including carding/age identification, safe storage to prevent theft, and safe marketing/shelving/display.
Wholesale distribution and sales limited to appropriately licensed businesses.
Distributors must be licensed to ensure hemp beverages are only distributed to properly licensed retailers that comply with 21+ retail sale requirements.
Distributors should reject suppliers not adhering to label requirements regarding age gating.
Where permitted by state law, businesses engaging in DTC sales should be subject to similar licensing requirements as physical retailers for 21+ compliance.

Online DTC retailers required to implement analogous industry standard protective measures (e.g., DTC alcohol sales) to ensure products are purchased by and delivered to adults aged 21 years or older.
Age-gating systems consistent with analogous industry standards must be in place for suppliers to sell directly to consumers.
Suppliers and retailers must not target or promote hemp beverages to people below the legal drinking age (LDA).
Restrictions apply to all marketing and advertising methods, including social media.
Marketing restrictions should reflect current restrictions for the alcohol industry.
Product labeling should not include designs, names, or features designed to appeal to children.

items.details: Hemp-specific licensure required for hemp ingredient providers, suppliers, distributors, and retailers within their operating states.

details: Manufacturers of hemp ingredients and beverages limited to state-licensed hemp manufacturers.
States should have an application process for manufacturers, including inspection procedures for food safety.
Licensed alcohol manufacturers (including breweries) should be allowed to produce hemp products.
Active state-licensed alcohol distributors (and cannabis, if applicable) should automatically receive hemp distributor licensure upon application.
Self-distributors, if permitted, should be subject to same taxation, quality control, and chain of custody protocols as third-party distribution.
Hemp beverage retail sales limited to state-licensed hemp retailers.
Active state-licensed alcohol (and cannabis, if applicable) retailers should automatically receive hemp retail licensure upon application.
Taxation systems must be in place to gather sales and excise tax revenue.
States should realize new tax revenue streams from the product category.
Reasonable excise tax structure (e.g., 3-5% of retail sale price) to fund regulatory and enforcement efforts.

items.details: Franchise agreements should be at the contract level and not incorporated into state law, allowing contracting parties to agree on terms.
